Individuals with Disabilities Education Act Section 618 · Parts B and C

IDEA Section 618 Data Tool

Explore national trends, State snapshots, side-by-side comparisons, and the full set of data collections on Children and Students with Disabilities served under IDEA, Part B and Part C, all drawn from the public data that every State reports each year to the U.S. Department of Education's Office of Special Education Programs under the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), Section 618.
IDEA Section 618 Data Tool

Chart descriptions (text)
Start here

Four ways into the data

Each year the U.S. Department of Education publishes data, under IDEA Section 618, on the children and students with disabilities that schools serve. This tool puts it all in one place. You don't need to be a data person to use it. Most people start with one of these:

✓Plain-language mode is on. Technical terms are shown in everyday words.

National Trends

Children and Students with Disabilities Served Under IDEA, Part B, Ages 3 Through 21, 1976–2025

This chart illustrates the consistent and significant growth in the number of Children and Students with Disabilities served under IDEA, Part B, ages 3 through 21, from School Year 1976–77 to today. Select one or more primary disability categories below to compare each category with the overall total.
Source
U.S. Department of Education, National Center for Education Statistics, Digest of Education Statistics 2023, Table 204.30; and Office of Special Education Programs, “IDEA Part B Child Count and Educational Environments Collection,” School Years 1976–77 through 2024–25.

Each Primary Disability Category at a Glance

A quick view of every primary disability category, showing the most recent count of children and students served and how much it has changed since the category was first reported under IDEA, Part B.
Source
U.S. Department of Education, Office of Special Education Programs, “IDEA Part B Child Count and Educational Environments Collection,” School Years 1976–77 through 2024–25.
Share of total

Each Category as a Share of the Total

Each selected primary disability category shown as its share of all Children and Students with Disabilities served under IDEA, Part B, ages 3 through 21, in School Year 2024–25.
Source
U.S. Department of Education, Office of Special Education Programs, “IDEA Part B Child Count and Educational Environments Collection,” 2024–25.
Change

Largest Changes in Count by Category

The primary disability categories with the largest increases and decreases in the number of children and students served, shown in thousands, through School Year 2024–25.
Source
U.S. Department of Education, Office of Special Education Programs, “IDEA Part B Child Count and Educational Environments Collection,” School Years 2000–01 and 2024–25.
Source: U.S. Department of Education, National Center for Education Statistics, Digest of Education Statistics 2023, Table 204.30; and Office of Special Education Programs, “IDEA Part B Child Count and Educational Environments Collection,” School Years 1976–77 through 2024–25.

Explore the data in depth

Each view focuses on a different part of the public IDEA Section 618 data that States and the additional reporting entities report to the Office of Special Education Programs (OSEP) each year. Select one to explore it in detail.

Disability Explorer

Choose one primary disability category to see how many Children and Students with Disabilities it serves, where they spend the school day, who they are, and how the numbers have changed over time. All figures cover Children and Students with Disabilities served under IDEA, Part B, ages 3 through 21, across the 50 States, the District of Columbia, and the additional IDEA reporting entities (Puerto Rico, the Bureau of Indian Education, the outlying areas, and the freely associated states).

Display
Trend

Child Count Trend, Served Under IDEA, Part B, Ages 3 Through 21

Number of Children and Students with Disabilities served under IDEA, Part B, each school year (ages 3 through 21), shown in thousands. Figures for School Years 2020–21 through 2024–25 use the official OSEP total rows (the 50 States, the District of Columbia, and the additional IDEA reporting entities); earlier years follow the published long-term series. The line traces how the count for the selected disability category has changed over time; switch to Percent above to view each year as a share of all Students with Disabilities served.
Environment

Educational Environments, Served Under IDEA, Part B, Ages 5 Through 21, School Year 2024–25

Where school-aged Children and Students with Disabilities in this category spend most of the school day, ages 5 through 21, in School Year 2024–25. Higher shares inside the regular class reflect more inclusive educational environments.
Demographics

Sex, Served Under IDEA, Part B, Ages 3 Through 21, School Year 2024–25

Children and Students with Disabilities served under IDEA, Part B, by reported sex.
Demographics

Race and Ethnicity, Served Under IDEA, Part B, Ages 3 Through 21, School Year 2024–25

Children and Students with Disabilities served under IDEA, Part B, by race and ethnicity.
Age

Age, Served Under IDEA, Part B, Ages 3 Through 21, School Year 2024–25

Children and Students with Disabilities served under IDEA, Part B, by single year of age.
Primary classification. These counts reflect each child's single primary disability category as recorded on the individualized education program (IEP), not clinical prevalence, and each child is counted only once. This applies to every category shown. For example, the autism category includes only children whose primary IDEA category is autism; it does not include every child with an autism diagnosis (a child served primarily under another category, such as intellectual disability or speech or language impairment, is counted there instead), nor children who are not served under IDEA, Part B.
Source: U.S. Department of Education, Office of Special Education Programs, IDEA Section 618 Child Count and Educational Environments Collection, School Year 2024–25 (the 50 States, the District of Columbia, and the additional IDEA reporting entities). The 50-year trend draws on the National Center for Education Statistics, Digest of Education Statistics 2023, Table 204.30, together with the Office of Special Education Programs 2023–24 and 2024–25 files. Counts, demographics, and ages cover Children and Students with Disabilities served under IDEA, Part B, ages 3 through 21; educational environments cover school-aged Students with Disabilities, ages 5 through 21. Figures are taken from the official OSEP national total rows.

State Snapshot

See how any State compares with the rest of the nation for its Children and Students with Disabilities served under IDEA, Part B. This page brings together the State's IDEA Section 618 data, the U.S. Department of Education's annual determination of how well the State meets IDEA requirements, an interactive U.S. map, and State-by-State rankings.

The snapshot reports, for the selected State, the number of Students with Disabilities served under IDEA, Part B, ages 3 through 21; how that count ranks among the 50 States and the District of Columbia; and the U.S. Department of Education's most recent annual determination. Each year the Department rates how well a State meets the requirements of IDEA as Meets Requirements, Needs Assistance, Needs Intervention, or Needs Substantial Intervention. Additional reporting entities (the outlying areas, the freely associated states, Puerto Rico, and the Bureau of Indian Education) are shown separately and are not included in the 50 + DC rankings.
SectionsSnapshotMapSection 618SPP/APR

Child Count Trend

IDEA Part B Child Count and Educational Environments Collection, ages 3–21.
Scope
The U.S. map and the State rankings cover the 50 States and the District of Columbia. Additional IDEA reporting entities, Puerto Rico, the Bureau of Indian Education, the outlying areas, and the freely associated states, appear in the selector when data are available, but they are shown separately and are not included in the 50 States and DC rankings. Each measure is labeled by collection, IDEA part, age group, and school year.

Map and Rankings

Choose one measure to color the map and rank the States for Children and Students with Disabilities served under IDEA, Part B.

U.S. Map,

Hover over any State to see its value. Click a State to load it into this page.

State Rankings

Source: U.S. Department of Education, Office of Special Education Programs, IDEA Section 618 collections.

Section 618 Measures

About these measures
Each measure is drawn from the IDEA Section 618 data collections and is labeled with its IDEA part, age group, and school year. Each tile shows this State's value, the national value, and the State's rank.

Measures by collection

How ranks are calculated
Ranks are out of the reporting units included for each measure. Most Part B child count and educational environment ranks use the 50 States and the District of Columbia. Graduation, dropout, and certification exclude New Mexico when data are suppressed for quality. Assessment proficiency is shown only in the assessment section and is not ranked here because state assessment systems are not directly comparable across states.

Trends, comparison & all States

About this section
How this State's published Part B measures compare with the nation, its child-count trend over time, and a sortable table of all States.

SEA Tables

Exact figures from the official IDEA Section 618 collections. Pick one or more collections, choose whether to compare States or disability categories, then add what you want to see side by side. Any table can switch between counts and percentages, turn into a chart with Visualize, or download as a CSV.

Every figure in these tables is an exact count reported to the U.S. Department of Education under IDEA Section 618, not an estimate. Switch any table between counts (the number of children and students) and percentages (a share of the total). A dash (–) marks a cell the Department suppressed to protect student privacy when very few students are involved.
Part B
Part C
Pick one or more
Compare by
Values
Reading the tables: what –, x, *, and 0 mean
Cells use the codes from the original source files: a dash (–) is not available, x and * are suppressed, and 0 is a reported zero. x suppressed, small cell size * suppressed, data-quality concerns – data not available The Office of Special Education Programs suppresses small or unreliable cells (x, *) to protect the privacy of individual students, and marks unavailable data with a dash (–). The U.S. row is OSEP's official published national total. It already includes every State and reporting entity, so suppressed cells in the State rows above do not reduce it, and it is not an undercount. Suppressed and unavailable cells in the individual State rows keep their source-file codes.

Part B LEA Explorer

Explore individual school districts, the Local Educational Agencies (LEAs) that serve Children and Students with Disabilities under IDEA, Part B. Choose a State to see all of its districts, then open any district for its full profile of Children and Students with Disabilities served and IDEA funds received. Child counts are School Year 2024–25; funding figures are School Year 2021–22.

Choose a state to begin

Pick a state to see all of its school districts, then open any district for its full profile. You can also click a state on the map below, or search a district by name up top.

Map · School Year 2024–25

Children and Students with Disabilities Served Under IDEA, Part B, by State

Darker = more Children and Students with Disabilities served under IDEA, Part B (ages 3 through 21). Click a State to load its districts below.
Source: U.S. Department of Education, Office of Special Education Programs. EDPass: “IDEA Part B Child Count and Educational Environments Collection,” 2024–25 (LEA file, specifications 002 and 089); and the IDEA Part B LEA Maintenance of Effort (MOE) Reduction and Coordinated Early Intervening Services (CEIS) collection, SY 2021–22. Suppressed cells are shown as “–”.

Part B Performance Measures

These are the official national performance measures for the IDEA Part B Grants to States program. They come from a federal law called the Government Performance and Results Act (GPRA), which asks every federal program to set a yearly target and then report the actual result. The figures below are taken directly from the U.S. Department of Education's yearly budget request to Congress for 2025 (a public document known as the Congressional Justification, which is where an agency explains and justifies the money it is asking for). Where a result has not been reported yet, it is shown as a dash (–) or marked TBD, never estimated.

Pick a topic to begin

Choose a topic above to see how IDEA Part B is doing against its national targets, or choose All measures to see every measure at once.

Source: U.S. Department of Education, Fiscal Year 2025 Budget Request, Special Education, State Grants: Grants to States, Program Performance Information (printed pp. 28–32). NAEP "students with disabilities" includes any student a school classifies as having a disability, including those served under a Section 504 plan. A dash (–) means data were not yet available when the budget was published; State-assessment results for 2020 were waived due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

Data Collections

The Office of Special Education Programs (OSEP), within the Office of Special Education and Rehabilitative Services (OSERS), is dedicated to improving results for infants, toddlers, children, and youth with disabilities, ages birth through 21, by providing leadership and financial support to assist States/entities, local districts, and programs. Section 618 of IDEA requires that each State/entity submit data about the infants and toddlers, birth through age 2, who receive early intervention services under Part C of IDEA, and Students with Disabilities, ages 3 through 21, who receive special education and related services under Part B of IDEA.

The full set of public IDEA Section 618 data collections, organized by reporting level, IDEA part, collection, and school year. Choose a collection to view its tables, charts, and original source files.

Browse IDEA Section 618 Collections

Choose a collection to view available tables, graphs, or source files.

Source note
U.S. Department of Education, Office of Special Education Programs: IDEA Part B Discipline, Exiting, Personnel, and Assessment collections, School Year 2023–24; IDEA Part C Child Count and Settings, School Year 2024–25; and IDEA Part C Exiting, School Year 2023–24.
Graphs
Charts and visual summaries for the selected collection, when available.
Part B Ages 3–21
Part B · Discipline · School Year 2023–24

Disciplinary Removals, Part B Discipline Collection, Ages 3 Through 21, School Year 2023–24

910,436 distinct Students with Disabilities (≈12%, about 1 in 8) had at least one disciplinary removal. Because a student can be removed more than once, the total number of removal events was far higher, about 2.24 million. This chart counts each student once, by their cumulative days removed. Per Office of Special Education Programs documentation: the unduplicated student count comes from file FS088/DG598; the 2.24M “times removed” figure from FS143/DG682.

Removal Event Measures

Selected event records from the discipline files.
Part B · Exiting · School Year 2023–24

Exiting Special Education, Part B Exiting Collection, Ages 14 Through 21, School Year 2023–24

How Students with Disabilities, ages 14 through 21, left special education in School Year 2023–24, shown as a percent of all students reported in the Exiting collection. Graduating with a regular high school diploma is the most common reason students exit.

Sources, data notes & suppression key
U.S. Department of Education · Office of Special Education Programs
IDEA Section 618 Data Products
Child count, educational environments, discipline, exiting, personnel, assessment, and the Part C collections, the core of this data tool (the 50 states, DC, and the additional IDEA reporting entities).
School Years 1976–77 through 2024–25
NCES
Digest of Education Statistics 2023
Historical national and state child-count series (Tables 204.30, 204.65, 204.70), drawn from the Office of Special Education Programs IDEA database.
1976–77 through 2022–23
NCES · NAEP
The Nation's Report Card (NAEP)
Long-Term Trend (ages 9 and 13) and Main NAEP results for students with disabilities compared with peers (national sample).
2012 through 2024
U.S. Census Bureau
American Community Survey, Age and Sex (S0101)
2024 1-year estimates of the U.S. population by age and sex, the denominator for the population comparisons (national).
2024
U.S. Department of Education · Office of Special Education Programs
2026 Determination Letters on State Implementation of IDEA
Annual Department determinations for State implementation of IDEA Part B and Part C, based on State Performance Plan and Annual Performance Report submissions. The state snapshot links both the Department page and the 2026 determinations fact sheet PDF.
Federal Fiscal Year 2024 submissions, issued 2026
About this data tool. The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) Section 618 requires every state to report data on infants, toddlers, children and youth with disabilities. There are 12 federal collections (8 under Part B, 4 under Part C). This data tool charts Child Count and Educational Environments in depth (School Years 1976–77 through 2024–25), plus headline views of Discipline, Exiting, Personnel, Assessment (Part B), and the Part C child-count, settings and exiting collections for the latest available year. The catalog above links every collection at data.ed.gov.
Data suppression. The Office of Special Education Programs suppresses small or unreliable cells. As defined in the source files: x suppressed, small cell size* suppressed, data-quality concerns– data not available National totals are taken from the official Office of Special Education Programs total rows; where a chart sums individual state-level cells, suppressed cells are treated as zero, so a few such totals are marginal undercounts; the count and type of suppressed cells appear beneath each affected chart.
Method and scope. National counts cover the 50 states, the District of Columbia, and the additional IDEA reporting entities (Puerto Rico, the Bureau of Indian Education, the outlying areas, and the freely associated states; Part C: the 50 states, DC, and the outlying areas), drawn from the official OSEP national total rows, ages 3–21 for Part B and birth–2 for Part C, unless noted. The State map, the State rankings, and the all-States comparison table cover the 50 states and the District of Columbia. In the long-term child-count trend, figures for School Years 2020–21 through 2024–25 use the official OSEP total rows (all reporting entities); historical figures through 2019–20 are from the NCES Digest of Education Statistics 2023, sourced from the Office of Special Education Programs IDEA database. School Year 2023–24 and 2024–25 figures and all other collections were computed directly from the Office of Special Education Programs IDEA Section 618 state-level files (extracted 2024–2025). “Percent of enrollment” reflects 2022–23, the latest year with a published denominator.
Sources: data.ed.gov · IDEA Section 618 Data Products · NCES 204.30 (by disability) · NCES 204.65 (environments) · NCES 204.70 (by state)
